erroneous_event: <error>“I know you’re in here, Fenn,” he called, voice flat, carrying through the cottage. “Your tracks weren’t subtle.”</error>
story:
Someone was in Cassius’s cottage. Fresh tracks wandered out of the trees and ended at his door, the snow only just beginning to fall since he’d shot the buck still warm on his shoulder. Retirement used to mean something, he growled. He dumped the deer, field dressed it with quick, practiced cuts, and hauled the remains to the root cellar. Steak after the intruder. Priorities.

He followed the prints, hand on his sword. Small steps. Uneven. Skidding. Sick or drunk, maybe. Or one of the mountain town whelps who thought bringing in Cassius’s head would buy them a new life. He pushed open his door—no ambush—and stepped in, pausing long enough to shed cloak and boots. He wasn’t an animal.

The supply room first. Chests big enough to hide a meager scrap of a kid. Empty. The stocked trunks, on a whim. Chaos. Apples with oranges, nuts with dried meat. Water gone. Bread and a single apple missing. Weapons, armor, potions untouched. So, not a hired blade. A hungry traveler, then. Maybe lost. He made a mental note to reorganize. After.

“I know you’re in here, Fenn,” he called, voice flat, carrying through the cottage. “Your tracks weren’t subtle.”

He swept each room, fingers brushing the sword’s grip. He’d barely crossed into the living room when something sprang from behind the chair. His blade flashed; he slapped the attacker with the flat, sent them tumbling over the table. He pinned the stranger’s back.

A boy. Thin as a crowbar, breath rattling. One shoe gone, the bare foot waxy and blue. Dirt in his hair, rags for clothes. Not a village whelp with full cheeks and a plan. This one looked like a strong gust might take him.

The boy’s eyes cut to Cassius, pupils pinpoints. “You,” he rasped.

“Me?” Cassius squinted. Even bounty hunters didn’t hate like that.

“I'm Fenn. Fenn Emric.”

Thorne’s boy. The king’s son. Cassius remembered a toddler with sticky fingers and a crown too big for his head. “He’s still breathing? Surprised he hasn’t been stabbed in his sleep,” Cassius muttered.

“This is all your fault!”

“My fault?” Cassius snapped. “My fault you broke into my home, stole my food, and jumped me? Shouldn’t you be wrapped in silk, eating grapes by the handful?”

The bite in his voice cost him. Fenn slipped free like an arrow and bolted—straight into the floorboards. He stumbled, cracked his head, and went still.

Cassius knelt fast, fingers at the throat. A pulse—thin, but there. He exhaled and hoisted the boy, lighter than a feed bag, onto the couch. “Karmic punishment,” he grumbled, already heading for blankets, bandages, potions. He soaked the wraps and worked with steady hands—bruises, burns, scrapes, neat and clinical. In the old days, when he wasn’t breaking bodies for the king, he’d stitched them.

He stacked wood, kindled a fire, and smothered Fenn in elk and bear hides to warm him slowly. The foot worried him. He’d seen frostbite turn flesh to coal. Better not to shock the system. Let the heat creep back.

The cottage filled with the crackle of flames and the smell of old smoke. Outside, snow kept falling. Inside, a prince lay swaddled on Cassius’s couch, starved and furious even in sleep. He looked worse than prisoners of war Cassius had fed on the march. Hard to believe royalty.

“Stew,” Cassius muttered. Warm, salty, heavy with meat. Good for a body that’s been starving. He stared toward the root cellar. “I’m going to waste my deer on this brat,” he said, and set about it anyway.
